title: AIS patrol-vessel detection from a coastal mast at Bazaruto Archipelago
summary: A 30 m coastal AIS receive mast at Vilanculos covers the Bazaruto Archipelago National Park's marine AOI — the vessel-tracking layer for coordinating the ranger patrol fleet, monitoring the artisanal-fisher exemption zone, and flagging unidentified incursions. Op B fixes the shore station and computes link viability for a class-B AIS tracker (`ais-tracker-162-vessel`) at every pixel of the marine AOI. Two-ray-over-water dominates: clutter class 80 (`Permanent water bodies`) returns 0 dB attenuation and the engine should select two-ray ground reflection rather than P.1812 over the open-water portion of each path, falling back to P.1812 for the inshore terrain-influenced segments.
demonstrates: Op B (§4.0) over a marine AOI
Two-ray-over-water propagation (clutter class 80 transparent; §4.5 model selection)

sites.name: shore-mast-vilanculos
sites.notes: 30 m self-supporting tower behind the harbor; long cable run to the receiver shack.

notes: Op B treats the shore mast as TX for symmetry; by RF reciprocity the coverage map is also the vessel-uplink coverage map for class-B AIS at this shore station. Most of the AOI is open water — expect long range with strong direct path plus the classic two-ray null/peak pattern as a function of TX/RX height and distance. The Bazaruto and Benguerra islands cause shadowing for vessels in their lee; those shadow zones are a known operational gap and the reason the park has historically also relied on patrol-boat AIS forwarders. Sensitivity class is deployment default (`org_internal` typically) — AIS is open broadcast and patrol-fleet positions are routinely shared with regional fisheries authorities.
